text: The surgeon begins the robotic ventral hernia repair by placing trocars, starting at Palmer's Point in the left upper quadrant beneath the rib cage. The camera port is positioned laterally for optimal overview, and an eight-millimeter trocar is placed in the left lower quadrant.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to dissect and establish a landing zone for the mesh, applying traction and counter traction while preserving the peritoneum. The edges of the fascia are scored, and a ruler is used to measure the area for mesh sizing.
The surgeon uses a Stratafix absorbable number one PDS suture in a running fashion to re-approximate tissue, demonstrating its strength and flexibility when manipulated by the robotic system.

The surgeon performs a corset-like suture technique to approximate and cinch down the tissue, gradually closing a large defect. The needle driver tips are used to advance the suture without excessive grabbing. The repair is executed in a double-layer fashion, with an initial re-approximation followed by an additional row of sutures.

The assistant palpates the hernia defect to ensure no fat or tissue is incarcerated before closure. The surgeon checks for potential back walling of the skin with the suture, especially in thin-walled patients. The hernia defect is now closed and measured.

The surgeon customizes a piece of polypropylene mesh, ensuring at least four to five centimeters of overlap on each side. A pocket is created to fit the mesh, and a double-arm PDS suture is used to secure it in place.

The surgeon uses a double-arm suture to secure the mesh in place, carefully going around the mesh to ensure it is held firmly. The other arm of the suture is then used to re-approximate the peritoneum, tying it to itself to complete the process.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to re-approximate the peritoneum, addressing a small defect with a portion of omentum visible below. The omentum is used to close the defect and cover the mesh, ensuring no direct contact with the non-coated mesh.

The surgeon inspects the surgical site to ensure the mesh and hernia defect are completely covered, confirming the closure of the hernia. The mesh is visibly covered by peritoneum and omentum, indicating a successful repair.

A vacuum dressing is applied over the surgical site with a four-by-four gauze placed over the defect. A needle is used to suction out the air, ensuring the dressing adheres closely to the skin. The dressing is intended to prevent seroma formation and is to be removed by the patient after three days.
